trend_name: Daisy Edgar-Jones and Sophie Turner Showcase Spring's "Glazed Pecan Brunette" Hair Trend
description:
Cool ash tones are officially out forspring, and in their place, a far warmer set ofhair colorsis coming to the forefront. Cue "glazed pecan brunette," an intensely flattering shade of brown that invites warm umber and copper hues to shine through without looking overly faux.
ActorsSophie TurnerandDaisy Edgar-Joneshave already co-opted the trend, both debuting glossy brown shades in the last week. While Daisy's take, which she showcased at last night's Burberry AW26 show in London, offers a subtle, bronzed update to her signature chocolate shade, Turner's brunette debut is a far cry from her natural blonde roots. What do the opposing references prove? This deep gilded tone looks good on absolutely everyone.
"A glazed pecan brunette is exactly what it sounds like: a rich, warm brunette elevated with an ultra-high gloss finish that gives the hair that glazed effect," explains hairdresserBrooke Evans, Quif Ambassador and owner of theGrid Collective. "The result is a multi-dimensional, expensive-looking color that feels soft, radiant, and incredibly polished. It's not flat or matte; it reflects light, creating that luminous sheen that makes the tone look alive."
She explains that the pecan element of the color is what brings warmth and richness, giving the brunette depth and a subtle golden warmth without tipping into copper.
"As we move into spring and head towards the summer months, clients naturally gravitate towards warmer, softer tones," she says. "After seasons of cooler brunettes and deeper winter shades, there’s a clear shift toward warmth and shine."
Danilo Giangreco, session stylist and owner ofDanilo Giangreco London, adds that the shade's growing popularity comes from a wider shift toward expensive-looking, low-maintenance color.
"People want dimension and gloss that grows out beautifully, rather than high-contrast highlights," he says. "This shade delivers warmth and luxury while still feeling natural and wearable. In the salon, ask for a soft brunette base with warm pecan or caramel tonal layering, paired with a gloss or glaze to enhance shine and seal the color. The key is delicate dimension, not chunky highlights to create reflection and fluidity."
It's important to note that while this shade has warmth at its base, it’s not copper or orange, says Evans.
"The warmth is secondary to the brunette, blended into the brown rather than sitting on top of it," she says. "The result should feel like rich chocolate with toasted warmth, not bright or brassy."
Fortunately, this fresh shade transcends a specific cut. Whether you typically lean into a more textured, choppy finish like Daisy's, a smooth, glossy finish like Sophie Turner's, or an airy set of curls, you're guaranteed to find the same flattering warmth regardless of your hair's natural texture or your skin tone. What could be more complementary for spring?

trend_name: Why the Angular Bob Is Trending Right Now
description:
It's well-established that there is now a short hairstyle for every single vibe, hair type, and texture; from tousledFrench bobsto longer mid-length chops that keep your ends looking fresh.
For those who love their suits extra sharp and their white shirts impeccably crisp, it's time to consider the angular bob. As the name suggests, the angular bob is the sharpest iteration of the short hairstyle. With a slightly graduated silhouette, the ends of the angular bob are trimmed with utmost precision to guarantee a pristine finish. It's a look that is meticulous and severe but undoubtedly cool, much like the look sported on the angular bob icon of the nineties,Victoria Beckham.
Recently, actressLily Collinstook a leaf out of Beckham's style file with her own version of the angular bob, styled by Gregory Russell for herEmily in Parispress tour. During her appearance on Tuesday'sTonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon,Collins met Beckham for the first time and admitted to channeling Beckham with her own razor-sharp bob, saying, "I hope I never leave my Posh era."
It was Vidal Sassoon who first originated this razor-sharp bob back in the Sixties. "The Sixties bob is a sleek, sculpted haircut that became a signature of the Mod era, largely thanks to Vidal Sassoon’s groundbreaking approach to precision cutting," Jake Unger, stylist atLarry Kingsalon in Marylebone, previously toldBazaar.
Since then, the cut has evolved to include face-framing pieces a la Beckham, deep side partings (as seen on model Jourdan Dunn), and with an extra glossy, mirror-like shine, like Collins.
It's a cut that works best for those with straighter locks, but, as with all bob haircuts, it can be easily tweaked to suit wavy and curly hair textures. Since it is a dramatic chop that speaks volumes, it's important to ensure a thorough consultation with your hairstylist before committing to it.
As expected, the angular bob will require strict maintenance to keep the silhouette looking immaculate. But given just how impactful the chop is, it's completely worth the time spent.

trend_name: The "Bubble Blowout" Is the One Fall Hair Trend You Need to Know
description:
If we could copy Amal Clooney’s shiny, impeccable hair every time she steps out, we probably would. The next best thing: Following the human rights lawyer’s go-to hairstylist,Dimitris Giannetos. It’s thanks to Giannetos that we learned Clooney recently showed off a chic new style, which the hairstylist called “the bubble blowout”—anddebuted a new haircut (but don't worry, she still has her enviable long hair).
“‘The bubble blowout’ and new chop for Amal Clooney!” Giannetos captioned an Instagram post last week, featuring Clooney onstage. Giannetos tellsBazaarhe cut a couple of inches of length from Clooney's signature long hair, which is currently an autumnal“pecan pie” hair color."We did a subtle chop to freshen things up. I took off about two inches to give her hair a healthier, more bouncy look," he says, adding, "That little trim made a big difference in how the blowout sat and helped amplify the volume."
The inspiration behind the "bubble blowout" was, Giannetos says, "to give her a glamorous, modern twist on the classic blowout."I wanted it to feel effortless yet polished, with that '90s sexy vibe but elevated for today. The goal was volume, movement, and that chic bubble-like illusion that feels playful but still red-carpet ready."
To get the look, Giannetos used a few key products: "I always prep with a serum before blow drying. For this look, I used theElvive Glycolic Gloss Serum by L’Oréal Paristo add shine without heaviness." Next was the most important part, the actual blowout: "I blow-dried using theL’Oréal Professional Air Light Prowith a small round brush, pinning each section as I went to set the shape." Finally, he kept everything in place with an iconic hairspray: "Once the hair cooled, I brushed it inwards to create that signature bubble effect and finished with plenty ofL’Oréal Paris Elnett Hairsprayfor hold and to tame flyaways," he says.
While the haircut is subtle, it's a fairly big change for Clooney, who keeps her look fairly consistent. We saw the style again this past weekend, as Clooney supported husband George at theLondon premiere of his filmJay Kelly. This time, she and Giannetos skipped the bubble blowout, instead going for a “vintage-glam” look that paired beautifully with her pink sequin gown and feather coat by Tamara Ralph. As always, her makeup was Charlotte Tilbury—andalsodone by Giannetos, a man of many talents.

I started using the Dyson Supersonic when it first came out in 2016, and I was initially amazed by how quickly it dried my hair. Unless I’m dilly-dallying, I can typically dry my hair within 10 minutes, though I’d imagine it might take a little longer if you use a biground brush.  Thanks to the tool's intuitive technology, I really don’t need to do much to have a good hair day. After waving it over my wet hair, my ambiguous waves become shinier, smoother, and more compliment-worthy. I’ve even convinced my mom, my sister, and several friends over the years to buy their own, and now, like me, they're hooked.
The Supersonic is also my frequenttravel companion, because the mere thought of settling for a lousy, hair-burning dryer terrifies me. I’ve owned the same hair dryer for almost 10 years and can vouch that it holds up nicely. One thing that makes this perfect hairdryer slightly less-than-perfect? Its price point, typically retailing for $450. But for a limited time, the expert-loved dryer is currently onsale for $300. If you’ve been curious to see what all the hype is about, now’s the time to snag one for yourself—I'm betting you won't be disappointed.

trend_name: How Does the Modern Sixties Bob Differ?
description:
As with most retro styles that have been revived for the new era (think'90s layers), today's sixties bob has a more relaxed feel that looks much less overdone. It's cool and lived-in, reflecting the beauty mood of the busy, jet-setting woman. It's also more versatile and respectful of individual hair textures.
"Modern sixties bobs are gentler around the edges, easier to wear, and far less rigid. This updated take also fits right into the 'old money' and 'quiet luxury' trends—clean, simple, understated, and effortlessly refined," Unger tellsBazaar UK. "Bangs are more versatile too, ranging from curtain and wispy shapes to softly grown-out fringes. Overall, the modern sixties bob is a luxurious evolution of a timeless classic."

trend_name: Who Should Try a Sixties Bob?
description: Anyone who's on the hunt for a polished bob should consider the sixties bob. It also has the bonus of creating the illusion of extra volume. "It works especially well on fine to medium hair, since the blunt edge naturally creates the look of thicker, healthier strands," Unger explains, adding that it looks equally elevated on wavy and curly hair.

This style makes an impactful statement that looks dressed up and elegant, even with minimal styling. Adds Unger: "The shape naturally adds structure and polish, making it a timeless choice that works in both casual and more dressed-up settings. It’s low maintenance and feels instantly elevated without being over-the-top. It’s the kind of cut that quietly upgrades your whole style."
Even better, the curvaceous silhouette and blunt edges give thin and fine hair extra body. "If your hair tends to look flat or wispy, a Sixties bob can make a noticeable difference without needing layers or heavy styling," explains Unger.

trend_name: How to Style a Sixties Bob
description:
For a glamorous, vintage vibe, Unger recommends a smooth blow-dry with the ends gently tucked in for that signature "sixties swing."
If you prefer to keep it cool and relaxed, air-dry with a volumizing mousse such asMy Nanna's Mousseby Larry King to encourage natural movement.
"If you want a playful retro touch, flipping the ends outward is a fun nod to the era, and if you have bangs, style them first; they set the tone for the whole shape," says Unger.
This party-ready bob is set to take over the festive season.

“Scalp scrubs are often too harsh, stripping, drying, and complicated to use, adding unnecessary steps to a wash-day routine,” Roszak tellsBazaar.“The majority of scrubs rely solely on physical exfoliation, causing the dryness and still not addressing the real cause of buildup.”
Rōz Salt Scalp Scrub, which officially launches on December 18, combines both physical and chemical exfoliation. I noticed the dual exfoliation right away, as I could go a day longer than usual without washing my hair, thanks to the deep (yet gentle) cleanse from the shampoo-meets-scrub. Plus, it has Himalayan and Bolivian salt, which run throughout the creamy formula—giving it a gorgeous pink hue—coconut-based surfactants to clean, and glycolic and salicylic acids to chemically exfoliate without stripping my keratin. The scrub also has a refreshing scent that transforms my shower into a spa. The biggest difference I noticed, aside from my less-frequent washes, was that my hair suddenly had substantial volume at the roots, likely due to the removal of buildup and oils.
Roszak says the color-and chemical-safe component is what drove her to launch the scrub. “There just wasn’t a scalp scrub that was effective and luxurious—gentle enough for regular use, safe for color-treated hair, and made with clean, high-performance ingredients,” she says. “This formula has lived behind my chair for years. I tested it on myself and dozens of clients long before it was an approved formula brought to market.”
Scalp scrubs aren’t new, but there is a new crop of gentle yet effective scrubs popping up, especially as scalp care has become a bigger priority lately. “We spent the last few years really focused on hair and scalp health,” celebrity hairstylistJustine Marjanpreviously toldBazaar.“Everything you do to care for your hair and scalp prior to styling really builds the foundation of any style, so it’s important to keep the end result in mind from the very start.” Not to mention, caring for the scalp can help stimulate growth, create healthy follicles (including helping stave offgray hairs), and prevent a dry, flaky scalp. Rōz’s scalp scrub contains caffeine and rosemary extract, both of which are linked to stimulating hair growth.
I’ve previously used Christophe Robin’s cult-favorite salt scrubs, also made with natural sea-salt crystals, which still reign high for me. Many formulas on the market now are not just scrubs but also exfoliating shampoos. I love Crown Affair’s Cleaning Scrub, which uses coconut surfactants like Rōz does to cleanse without stripping and lathers beautifully, and Anablue’s Scalp Cleanser, a scrub with pink Himalayan sea salt and argan oil that surprisingly doesn’t leave hair greasy or weighed down. Ahead, my other favorite scalp-scrub picks that are worth adding to your routine.

trend_name: I Can’t Stop Thinking About the Chrome Hair on the Chanel Runway
description:
It girls everywhere have been clamoring to buyChanelcreative directorMatthieu Blazy’sdebut Spring 2026 collection in Paris since it became available for purchase mere days ago. While I’m also plotting how to literally secure the bag (any of them, really) from last season, the maison’s chrome-accented hair and makeup looks from the new Fall/Winter 2026 collection have quickly become my new obsession, and I’m not the only one.
“It was so, so incredible—glitter extensions in a chignon!”Bazaarexecutive digital director Lynette Nylander said of the season’s styling after attending the show in person. And even from afar, all the way in New York City, the surprise appeal of metallic beauty accents on the runway wasn’t lost on me.
Led by celebrity hairstylistDuffy, models at Chanel’s fall/winter presentation wore a range of looks, like sleek blowouts, loose waves, natural coils, and preciseslicked-back buns. But a handful of outfits from the collection were punctuated by standout silver chignons, some with a heavy dusting of glitter and others with subtle purple ombré and intense tinsel extensions woven into aFrench twist. The updos were only part of the collection’s beauty color story, as they were paired with a wash of matchingsilver eyeshadowon the lid and a glossy lip. A handful of models also wore wavy back-grazing silver wigs with soft streaks of purple and blue throughout, with sparklinglavender shadowas a final touch.
In a sea of minimal, barely there hair moments, Duffy and Blazy’s unexpected pops of chrome and glitter were a welcome addition to high-fashion runways. It also begs a question: Is silver hair a key trend for fall 2026? If Oscar nomineeTeyana Taylor’s sleek and silver hairdo at the showis any indication, cool-tone shades are about to have a major beauty moment.

If nourishing your hair was important before, it’s even more important as we age. “Gray hair needs that because there are textural changes that are inherently associated with the lack of pigment and the lack of the disulfide bonds,” explains Small, referring to the chemical bonds that maintain the structure of hair’s keratin protein.
Small says he views gray hair like a balloon that’s starting to deflate. “I want to fill it up with moisture and nourishing ingredients,” he explains.
Luckily, there are plenty of products that can help in this area: “Rich conditioners, weekly masks, and a good leave-in are key,” says Santiago. Her favorite?AGE beautiful Hydrating Mask, which "rehydrates and moisturizes wiry, coarse silver hair."

“I think many of us have adopted a more robust skincare routine as we've gotten older, and then shampoo and conditioner kind of remains the same,” says Small. He stresses the importance of a basic knowledge of ingredients and formulas. “If you have gray hair that's coarse or dry or brittle, if something's too cleansing, too stripping, that can be a very easy place to start,” he says. “There are now a lot of ingredients within shampoo that we can look for with antioxidants andpeptides that will nourish the scalpas well.”

On that same token, there are certain ingredients to avoid, like sulfates, which act as a surfactant, the agent that causes shampoo to lather up. According to Small, the best shampoo for gray hair may not be quite as sudsy as the ones you're used to—and that’s OK. “They’re potentially not the best thing for hair and for our scalps, and that might mean less suds,” he says.
For conditioners, he recommends going silicone-free. “There are a lot of great natural oils, extracts and alternatives. Sunflower oil, jojoba oil, and shea butter are great materials that will absorb into a hair strand and won't coat the hair strand like silicones do.” He adds that while “there are some good versions” of silicones, “the majority of them sit on the surface, they weigh the hair down, and they make hair feel greasy.”

“The scalp gets drier with age, so gentle exfoliation, lightweight hydrating serums, and a little massage help with circulation and follicle health,” says Santiago.
Small also recommends gentle exfoliation, noting that anything too harsh could affect the scalp’s microbiome. “We make one that is a salicylic, glycolic, lactic acid mixture, but without the abrasion,” he says of the Scrub by Arey.

trend_name: Treat Yourself to Regular Scalp Massages
description:
Both experts stress the importance of a good scalp massage to stimulate circulation. “Scalp massages are really important, whether it's with your fingertips or a dry brush,” says Small. That being said, he says to be extra careful—especially when the scalp is wet—to avoid excess tugging or pulling.
Also, use a scalp serum for bonus benefits, like Dr. Barbara Sturm Super Anti-Aging Scalp Serum, which has scalp- and hair-strengthening ingredients like hyaluronic acid and vitamin B5.

Cutting your hair is one of the best ways to ensure hair health, no matter your color. Plus, it just helps gray hair look polished. “Regular trims and gentle styling can help prevent frizz and maintain an intentional look,” says Santiago.

UV rays also affect your hair, and of course, your scalp. “Don’t forget sunscreen on your part,” reminds Santiago. “Gray hair reflects more UV, and the scalp burns faster.” For extra credit, wear a hat when you're in direct sunlight.

Gray hair tends to have a different texture and finish than other types of hair, so don’t be surprised if your styling routine needs a rethink, too. And that might mean more heat-styling (with proper prep and heat protection, of course): “If you used to wear your hair natural and let it dry naturally, you might need to polish it a little bit more,” says Small.

trend_name: Use a Purple Shampoo and Conditioner
description: Gray color can look dull, too—and that’s where a purple shampoo can work wonders to brighten it up. Santiago stresses to “Protect hair from the sun, use neutralizing shampoos, and add a clear or silver gloss for shine” for bright gray or white color, recommending Clairol Shimmer Lights to get the job done.

If graying or thinning happens suddenly, it might be time to check in with your doctor in addition to your trusted hairstylist. “What graying indicates is that there's a change happening in the body,” says Small. “Typically, we look at it as a barometer—whether it's nutrition, active stress, general age, stress in general, sleeping, et cetera.”
Santiago concurs, adding that “managing stress, supporting your nutrition, and avoiding chronic heat or harsh chemicals can slow the process and keep your hair healthy longer.”
